title: Why spending compute at inference raises accuracy

beats.text: Training is not the only place to spend compute; a frozen model gets more accurate when you let it run longer or more times.

visual.sub: A second compute budget after training

beats.text: A language model is a distribution, not a function, so sampling a hard problem many times gives a spread of candidate answers.
Coverage is the chance the best of N independent samples is correct, equal to one minus the probability all N draws fail.

visual.note: p is per-sample success; N is the number of samples

beats.text: The curve is concave: the first few samples buy the most accuracy, then returns diminish geometrically toward the ceiling.
With a per-try probability of 0.3 and five samples, coverage climbs to about 0.83, but only if you can pick the right one.
Coverage is an upper bound, not accuracy; knowing a correct answer is somewhere in N samples is useless unless you can identify it.

title: Sampling-based scaling: best-of-N and majority vote

beats.text: The simplest way to turn coverage into accuracy needs no extra model: sample N answers and aggregate them.
Self-consistency samples N chains of thought, extracts each final answer, and returns the most frequent one.

visual.note: w_i = 1 for plain majority, or a score for weighted voting

beats.text: Given five values of 13, 13, 7, 13, and 9, the mode is 13 with three of five votes, so majority vote returns 13.
Voting works because correct reasoning paths converge while wrong ones disperse, so the modal answer is usually right.
By the Condorcet jury theorem, when the correct answer is most probable, majority accuracy rises toward one with N.

visual.label: two-way threshold for voting to help

beats.text: When probability is low or split across distractors, voting saturates below the best-of-N oracle, and that gap is what a verifier recovers.

title: Verifier-guided selection: outcome vs process rewards

beats.text: A verifier is a learned model that scores candidate solutions, bridging the gap from coverage to delivered accuracy.
An outcome reward model scores only the final answer, while a process reward model scores each reasoning step.

visual.note: PRM multiplies per-step scores, so one bad step tanks the chain

beats.text: Process supervision multiplies per-step scores, so a three-step chain at 0.9, 0.7, and 0.667 earns an overall score of 0.42.
Setting the verifier to one recovers plain voting, and a perfect verifier recovers the oracle, so quality is the dial between them.
Push N high enough and best-of-N starts reward-hacking, selecting answers that exploit the verifier's blind spots.
A verifier is only as trustworthy as its weakest covered case, and gains above the voting line are borrowed against its reliability.
Search expands a solution incrementally, scoring partial states and steering generation toward promising branches.
Beam search keeps the B highest-scored partial solutions at each step, expands each, re-scores, and prunes back to B.
A step-level PRM makes search possible because it scores prefixes, letting you prune dead branches before paying to finish them.
Lookahead rolls the model forward a few steps to estimate a branch, while MCTS balances exploring against exploiting known-good lines.
Snell and colleagues found the best test-time strategy depends on difficulty: cheap refinement for easy, search for hard.

visual.label: larger model best-of-N can sometimes match

beats.text: Search inherits the verifier's flaws and can collapse diversity, leaving it below simple sampling on some problems.

visual.note: search climbs the PRM and can over-rate flawed lines

title: Thinking longer: long chain-of-thought & budget forcing

beats.text: Instead of many parallel samples, the 2024 to 2025 shift spent compute sequentially in one very long chain of thought.
The o1 and DeepSeek-R1 paradigm trains one model to plan, explore, check itself, and backtrack within a single generation.
Accuracy on reasoning benchmarks grows roughly linearly in the log of the thinking-token budget B, with cost linear in B.

visual.note: doubling tokens buys a roughly constant accuracy increment

beats.text: With a = 0.50 and b = 0.02 per doubling, a budget of 4096 tokens is twelve doublings, reaching an accuracy of 0.74.
Each doubling of the budget adds a fixed increment, so accuracy grows in log-B while compute grows in B itself.
Budget forcing steers thinking at decode time by suppressing the stop token and appending Wait, but it saturates and can degrade.

title: Compute-optimal scaling and the cost of thinking

beats.text: The real question is where a fixed FLOP budget should go: a bigger model, more test-time samples, or longer thinking.

visual.sub: Where every FLOP returns the most accuracy

beats.text: Total inference cost scales with model size times tokens generated, about 2P FLOPs per token times N times L.

visual.note: P parameters, N samples, L tokens per sample

beats.text: On easy-to-moderate problems at modest budgets, a small model plus test-time compute can match a model about 14 times larger.
The optimum is not constant; it slides toward many samples on easy problems and toward a bigger model on hard ones.
With a perfect verifier at p = 0.5 and four samples, pass@4 is 0.9375, so cost per solved task is 4.27 units.
Both axes show diminishing returns, so production systems route by difficulty, taking the expensive path only when it earns its keep.
